topic: An employee finds out a junior colleague makes more money
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the office hummed with an aggressive monotony, mirroring the churning in Sarah’s stomach. She stared at the spreadsheet, the numbers blurring into meaningless ink. When the quarterly compensation review landed in her inbox, the discrepancy was stark, a gaping chasm between her projected earnings and what Mark, a junior analyst barely out of his training, was clocking. Sarah felt a sudden, hot flush creep up her neck. She gripped her mouse so tightly her knuckles turned white, desperately trying to focus on the task at hand, but every glance toward Mark felt like a physical assault. She mumbled something about needing a moment, her voice thin and reedy, and retreated to her cubicle, the silence amplifying the frantic thumping in her chest.

[story 2]

"It’s just… I don’t understand," Liam muttered, the words catching in his throat as he looked at the pay stub. The contrast was undeniable. He felt a strange, cold pressure building behind his eyes. He kept re-reading the figures, the difference seeming monumental, an insult etched in currency. When his supervisor called him into his office, Liam managed a strained smile, forcing his shoulders back. He spoke carefully, his tone overly polite, avoiding any direct confrontation. Every syllable felt heavy, weighed down by the realization of his own perceived inadequacy. He spent the rest of the afternoon meticulously organizing files, his movements stiff and overly precise, a visible effort to mask the internal turmoil boiling just beneath the surface.

[story 3]

Maya picked up her coffee mug, the ceramic warm against her palms, but the warmth offered no comfort. The data report had been irrefutable, and the reality of the pay gap settled over her like a suffocating blanket. She watched Mark effortlessly navigate the budget discussion in the meeting, his confidence radiating outward. Maya felt a strange tightening in her chest, a desperate need to shrink into her chair. When the manager praised Mark’s contribution, Maya offered a weak nod, her gaze fixed on the floor tiles. She deliberately kept her hands folded in her lap, a silent testament to her own feeling of being utterly outmatched, wishing the floor would simply swallow her whole.
